jokes.text: Student questioning cafeteria worker about meat recycling: 'So is it the same meat? Are you guys recycling the meat?' 'It's different meat.' 'Yeah, well... I guess you would have to say that, right?'
jokes.setup: Student notices suspicious pattern of different meat dishes served consecutively
jokes.punchline: Deadpan observation that cafeteria worker would have to deny recycling meat regardless
jokes.explanation: Sharp observational humor with good structure. The deadpan 'you would have to say that' is economical and clever.

jokes.text: 'Excellent choice, sir' - lunch lady's sarcastic response
jokes.setup: Student just accused her of serving recycled meat
jokes.punchline: She treats his order like he's at a fancy restaurant
jokes.explanation: Great economy and perfect cafeteria worker attitude

jokes.text: Daniel: 'Some Salisbury dog meat for us, please.'
jokes.setup: Previous conversation about questionable meat quality
jokes.punchline: Casual reference to dog meat when ordering
jokes.explanation: Good callback that builds on the meat recycling joke, fits Daniel's character as a burnout who'd make dark jokes.

jokes.text: Lindsay: 'You're kinda stoned.' Nick: 'I'm very stoned.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay makes understatement about Nick's condition
jokes.punchline: Nick's matter-of-fact correction to stronger degree
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy and character work. Nick's blunt honesty about his stoned state is both funny and character-defining.

jokes.text: Mark's dad destroying grow lights: 'My dad found my grow lights, and he ran over them with his car.' 'He better have.'
jokes.setup: Discussion about getting marijuana
jokes.punchline: Absurdly aggressive parental response of running over equipment with car
jokes.explanation: The image of a dad methodically running over grow lights with his car is absurd and memorable.

jokes.text: Harold struggling with TV channel selection - multiple failed attempts
jokes.setup: Dad trying to change channels on remote
jokes.punchline: Multiple failed attempts with frustrated sighs
jokes.explanation: Generic dad-with-technology humor, predictable and low-effort

jokes.text: Harold: 'Sam, who's your favorite Angel?' Sam: 'I like Bosley.' Harold: 'Bosley? Nobody watches this show for Bosley.'
jokes.setup: Father asking about Charlie's Angels preferences
jokes.punchline: Sam chooses the least attractive male character
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment showing Sam's innocence/naivety. Harold's incredulous response is genuinely funny.

jokes.text: Harold's double standard: 'I just think there comes a time in a young man's life when he should begin testing the waters.' Lindsay: 'What about me testing the waters?' 'You can test the waters, too--after you get married.'
jokes.setup: Father encouraging Sam to date
jokes.punchline: Immediate 180 when Lindsay asks about dating
jokes.explanation: Classic parental double standard joke executed with perfect timing. Very relatable and character-specific.

jokes.text: 'The Cindy ship has sailed. It's time to board another vessel.'
jokes.setup: Sam still pining over Cindy who's with someone else
jokes.punchline: Harold's nautical metaphor for moving on romantically
jokes.explanation: Decent dad advice with mildly clever nautical extended metaphor

jokes.text: Neal's metaphor: 'The Cindy Sanders of this world are just...hors d'oeuvres. Vicki is a 5-course meal.' Sam: 'Horwhat?'
jokes.setup: Neal trying to sound sophisticated with food metaphor
jokes.punchline: Sam's complete confusion at basic French term
jokes.explanation: Great character work showing Neal's pretensions and Sam's innocence. The mispronunciation is perfectly delivered.

jokes.text: 'Hey, Bill. Bill, you want some crushed nuts with your sundae?' Bill: 'My what?' 'OHH!'
jokes.setup: Bully making innuendo about 'crushed nuts'
jokes.punchline: Bill's innocent confusion followed by apparent physical comedy
jokes.explanation: Juvenile bullying humor that works more for cringe than genuine laughs. Fairly predictable setup.

jokes.text: Bill: 'That guy needs a new joke.'
jokes.setup: After enduring the bully's repetitive humor
jokes.punchline: Bill's matter-of-fact critique of the bully's material
jokes.explanation: Brilliant deadpan response. Bill essentially doing comedy criticism on his own bully is both funny and character-perfect.

jokes.text: Bill's allergy list escalation: 'Well, a lot of things are on that list--like, bees and air and cats and dogs. Well, not dogs. Well, some dogs. If I eat a peanut, I could die.'
jokes.setup: Teacher asking about Bill's allergies
jokes.punchline: Increasingly absurd list culminating in deadly peanut allergy
jokes.explanation: Great escalating structure with good character work. The self-corrections ('Well, not dogs. Well, some dogs') feel very real.

jokes.text: Alan: 'So don't eat 'em, dork.' Bill: 'I don't. If I did, I could die.'
jokes.setup: Bully's obvious advice about avoiding peanuts
jokes.punchline: Bill's literal-minded response missing the sarcasm
jokes.explanation: Bill's earnest response to sarcasm is character-appropriate and mildly amusing.

jokes.text: Bill oversharing: 'Like...like, Neal has psoriasis, which is like lizard skin. And Sam passes out if he even thinks about blood.'
jokes.setup: Bill trying to normalize his allergies
jokes.punchline: Accidentally humiliating his friends with personal details
jokes.explanation: Perfect cringe comedy. Bill's well-meaning overshare that destroys his friends is painfully funny and very character-specific.

jokes.text: Neal's psoriasis defense: 'Hey. It's called psoriasis, and 10% of the population will suffer from it at some point in their life. It's nothing to be ashamed of.'
jokes.setup: Bill's embarrassing revelation about Neal's skin condition
jokes.punchline: Neal's overly clinical, defensive response
jokes.explanation: Neal's nerdy, statistical approach to defending his embarrassing condition is very in character.

jokes.text: Neal's detailed psoriasis statistics
jokes.setup: Bill just embarrassed him about his skin condition
jokes.punchline: Neal responds with clinical statistics instead of anger
jokes.explanation: Perfect Neal response - deflecting embarrassment with nerdy facts

jokes.text: Sam vs Bill romantic prospects: 'I got a better chance with Miss Foote than you have with Vicki, and I don't have a chance at all.'
jokes.setup: Argument about romantic prospects
jokes.punchline: Sam's logic that proves both their hopelessness
jokes.explanation: Excellent structure and economy. Sam's self-aware logic is both funny and painfully accurate for teenage dynamics.

jokes.text: Daniel on Nick's pot sharing: 'You haven't passed a joint in your life.'
jokes.setup: Nick claiming Daniel should share pot because Nick always hooks him up
jokes.punchline: Daniel's blunt contradiction of Nick's claim
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing the dynamics of their drug-centered friendship. Daniel's directness is funny.

jokes.text: Nick's desperation: 'I almost smoked my mom's ferns last night.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay asking how Nick is handling being without pot
jokes.punchline: Absurd image of smoking houseplants out of desperation
jokes.explanation: Perfectly absurd and economical. The image of someone so desperate they'd smoke decorative plants is both funny and sad.

jokes.text: Teacher's Bill peanut joke: 'And for being Bill's mortal enemy. Because get it, Bill? You're allergic to peanuts.'
jokes.setup: Teaching about George Washington Carver and peanuts
jokes.punchline: Teacher making obvious connection to Bill's allergy
jokes.explanation: Weak teacher humor that's more cringe than funny. The over-explanation kills any potential impact.

jokes.text: Bill: 'You know, Bill, you're not a geek.' 'Tell that to the rest of the world.'
jokes.setup: Teacher trying to be encouraging
jokes.punchline: Bill's realistic assessment of his social status
jokes.explanation: Bill's self-aware response to well-meaning but naive encouragement is both funny and poignant.

jokes.text: Gordon's sci-fi enthusiasm: 'I don't like sci-fi. I love, love, love it!'
jokes.setup: Discussion about sci-fi convention
jokes.punchline: Gordon's extreme enthusiasm correction
jokes.explanation: Good character work showing Gordon's intense nerd enthusiasm. The escalation works for his character type.

jokes.text: Nick's natural argument: 'It's from the earth. It's natural. Why would it be there if we weren't supposed to smoke it?' Lindsay: 'Dog crap is here, and we don't smoke that.'
jokes.setup: Nick making natural fallacy argument for marijuana
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's perfectly logical counter-example
jokes.explanation: Brilliant logical deconstruction delivered with perfect economy. Lindsay's response completely deflates Nick's stoner philosophy with unassailable logic.

jokes.text: Nick's stoned music appreciation: 'Oh, shhh... listen to this drum fill. Ohh! That guy is amazing. Do you know how hard that is?'
jokes.setup: Nick stoned and listening to music
jokes.punchline: His intense focus on minor musical details
jokes.explanation: Accurate portrayal of stoned behavior - the intense focus on trivial musical details many can relate to.

jokes.text: Lindsay's pot criticism vs Nick's defense: 'What's the highlight of your week? Sitting on your couch eating chips, dreaming of Drakes Cakes?' 'It's better than sitting there with a board up my butt, like you.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay criticizing Nick's lifestyle
jokes.punchline: Nick's crude but effective counter-attack
jokes.explanation: Good escalating argument with character-appropriate insults. Nick's 'board up my butt' is crude but effective.

jokes.text: Nick's dramatic pot disposal: 'Here, you see it? I'm really addicted. How am I gonna live without it?' [flushes pot]
jokes.setup: Lindsay calling Nick addicted
jokes.punchline: Nick sarcastically proving his non-addiction by flushing his stash
jokes.explanation: Good dramatic gesture that proves Lindsay's point while Nick thinks it disproves it. Physical comedy enhances the moment.

jokes.text: Peanut sandwich investigation: 'This is crunchy.' 'So?' 'Like...like peanuty crunchy.'
jokes.setup: Bill eating sandwich, audience knows it contains peanuts
jokes.punchline: Bill's dawning realization of the danger
jokes.explanation: Good tension building with dramatic irony. The audience knows what's coming, making Bill's realization both funny and suspenseful.

jokes.text: Hospital word choice: 'Could you not use the word "die" in a hospital?' 'You asked if he was still alive.' 'That's an optimistic question. Alive is good. Die is bad. Trust me on this. My dad's a dentist.'
jokes.setup: Discussion about appropriate hospital language
jokes.punchline: Sam's irrelevant dentist credential as medical authority
jokes.explanation: Sam's complete non-sequitur about his dentist dad as medical authority is absurd and character-appropriate.

jokes.text: Floyd confusion: 'I saw them at the Pyramids.' 'The Egyptian Pyramids?' 'Uh, I don't think they've ever played the Pyramids.' 'Oh, yeah. You're right. It was the Dead.'
jokes.setup: Burnout graduate talking about concerts
jokes.punchline: Confused mix-up of bands and impossible venues
jokes.explanation: Good portrayal of burned-out former student whose memories are scrambled. Realistic stoner confusion.

jokes.text: Ken's laser dome experience: 'I saw Floyd at the laser dome. They weren't there, but it was their music.'
jokes.setup: Frank's confused concert stories
jokes.punchline: Ken's matter-of-fact description of obviously not a concert
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan delivery of absurd concept. Ken's literal interpretation of 'seeing' a band at a planetarium show is brilliant.

jokes.text: Russo's rhetorical question: 'How dumb do I look?' Daniel: 'Do you really want to know?'
jokes.setup: Principal asking rhetorical question
jokes.punchline: Daniel taking it literally and offering honest assessment
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment. Daniel's willingness to honestly answer an obviously rhetorical question is both ballsy and funny.

jokes.text: Ghost Bill friendship: 'Maybe he'd be like Casper the Friendly Ghost. He'd hang out with us, and no one would talk to him but us... and people would catch us talking to him, and they would think we were crazy--like Wilbur on Mr. Ed.'
jokes.setup: Discussing what would happen if Bill died and became a ghost
jokes.punchline: Comparing their situation to Mr. Ed's Wilbur
jokes.explanation: Sweet and nerdy speculation with good pop culture reference. The logic is both funny and touching.

jokes.text: Stoner existential crisis: 'What do people do when they're not stoned?' 'I don't know.' 'Relate to one another?'
jokes.setup: Nick realizing he doesn't know how to function sober
jokes.punchline: His uncertainty about basic human interaction
jokes.explanation: Brilliant existential moment that's both funny and sad. Nick's genuine confusion about sober human interaction is perfectly delivered.

jokes.text: Lindsay's excuse: 'I have... woman problems.' Harold: 'I don't want to hear about it.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay needing excuse while high
jokes.punchline: Harold's immediate retreat from female biology
jokes.explanation: Classic parental avoidance humor. Harold's immediate shutdown is both funny and character-appropriate.

jokes.text: Millie's drug awareness: 'You're high.' 'What?!' 'You're on the pot.' 'How could you tell?' 'I'm not dumb, Lindsay. I know what high people look like. I went to a Seals and Crofts concert last summer.'
jokes.setup: Millie identifying Lindsay's condition
jokes.punchline: Her unlikely source of drug knowledge
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment. Straight-laced Millie's drug knowledge from the most innocent possible source is brilliantly unexpected.

jokes.text: Millie's moral stance: 'Fine. I'll go with you. But only for the safety of the child.' Lindsay: 'Thank you, Millie.' 'No, thank your dealer.'
jokes.setup: Millie agreeing to help with babysitting
jokes.punchline: Her sarcastic final comment about Lindsay's situation
jokes.explanation: Great character moment showing Millie's moral stance while still being sarcastic. The 'thank your dealer' line is perfectly delivered.

jokes.text: Alan's childhood memory: 'I remember, like, in the fourth grade, I used to think you guys were, like, really cool. And this one time, you brought a model of the Saturn 5 in for show-and-tell, and I asked you guys if I could shoot off rockets with you, and you said no.'
jokes.setup: Alan apologizing to unconscious Bill
jokes.punchline: Revelation of childhood rejection over model rockets
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character development that recontextualizes Alan's bullying. The specific detail about the Saturn 5 rocket makes it feel authentic and poignant.

jokes.text: Jean's pregnancy confession: 'When I was pregnant with Sam, I didn't think twice about nutrition. I was drinking a lot back then... getting high, poppin' pills.'
jokes.setup: Jean feeling guilty about Bill's allergies
jokes.punchline: Casual revelation of drug use during Sam's pregnancy
jokes.explanation: Dark humor from unexpected source. Jean's casual admission creates uncomfortable comedy that fits the show's tone.

jokes.text: Jean's baby dropping confession: 'When Sam was 4 months old... I... dropped him on a brick patio. He had a hairline fracture of the skull.'
jokes.setup: Previous confession about pregnancy drug use
jokes.punchline: Even worse revelation about dropping baby Sam
jokes.explanation: Escalation of Jean's inappropriate hospital confessions. Dark humor that explains Sam's fainting condition.

jokes.text: Millie's faith vs Lindsay's logic: 'I don't believe in God.' 'I know. That's why you're unhappy. That's why you're stoned.' 'God doesn't make sense. It's not logical.' 'Well, I may not be logical, but I have faith.'
jokes.setup: Stoned Lindsay questioning existence
jokes.punchline: Millie's simple faith versus Lindsay's drug-fueled logic
jokes.explanation: Perfect character contrast. Millie's simple faith versus Lindsay's complicated nihilism creates both humor and pathos.

jokes.text: Lindsay's existential dog dream: 'What if this is a dream, and it's not even our dream, it's that dog's dream. Maybe we're just existing in his mind, and all of a sudden he'll wake up to go drink out of the toilet, and we'll be gone.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay having stoned philosophical thoughts
jokes.punchline: Absurd theory about existence being a dog's dream
jokes.explanation: Classic stoner philosophy taken to absurd extremes. The specific detail about toilet water makes it funny rather than pretentious.

jokes.text: Bill's hospital revelation: 'Maureen and Vicki showed up, and they were crying.' 'Really? Because of me?' 'Yeah. They were really worried.' 'Well, if you want, you can tell them that I'm still dying.'
jokes.setup: Bill learning girls were worried about him
jokes.punchline: His willingness to fake continued illness for attention
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bill moment showing his pragmatic approach to social advancement. Dark but character-appropriate humor.

jokes.text: Food scientist appreciation: 'This is so good. You know why this is so good? Because it was made by food scientists.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay eating junk food while high
jokes.punchline: Her pseudo-intellectual explanation for why processed food tastes good
jokes.explanation: Good stoner logic humor. Lindsay's attempt to intellectualize junk food is appropriately ridiculous.

jokes.text: Alan's aggressive caring: 'I knew you wouldn't die. I can't believe you'd be such a wuss to die from a peanut.' 'Aren't you glad I didn't die?' 'Yeah, I'm glad for my sake. Think I want to end up in juvie for killing you?'
jokes.setup: Alan visiting recovered Bill
jokes.punchline: His self-centered reason for being glad Bill survived
jokes.explanation: Perfect Alan character moment. His concern is purely self-interested but delivered with typical bully logic.

jokes.text: Bill's manipulation: 'You care about me. I heard everything you said.' 'I don't care about you.' 'Yeah, you do. You think I'm cool. You want me to pound you?'
jokes.setup: Bill using Alan's private confession against him
jokes.punchline: Turning the tables on his former bully
jokes.explanation: Excellent power reversal. Bill using Alan's vulnerable moment against him is both funny and satisfying character growth.

jokes.text: Gordon's parking lot costume strategy: 'I put everything on in the parking lot. You know, that way I don't look like a loser all day.'
jokes.setup: Others asking about Gordon's costume absence
jokes.punchline: His strategic approach to nerd shame management
jokes.explanation: Perfect nerd logic. Gordon's practical approach to managing social embarrassment is both funny and relatable.

jokes.text: Alan's costume crisis: 'Oh, man. I just can't do it.'
jokes.setup: Alan about to put on costume for sci-fi convention
jokes.punchline: The bully can't bring himself to embrace full geek identity
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Alan's limits. Even after bonding with the geeks, he can't fully embrace their world.
